content.text: Pre-training teaches a base language model
general knowledge and language patterns

from large unlabeled text before any agent-specific tuning. The objective chosen (causal LM, masked LM, etc.) defines what the model learns to predict—and therefore what raw capabilities an agent stack starts from.
What it is
Pre-training objectives map input sequences to prediction targets:
Causal language modeling (CLM)
— predict next token given past tokens; used by GPT, LLaMA, agent chat models
Masked language modeling (MLM)
— predict hidden tokens from bidirectional context; BERT-style
Span corruption
— T5 predicts missing spans; encoder–decoder pre-training
Multi-token prediction
— auxiliary heads predict several future tokens; emerging in some modern stacks
Why it matters

Agent products rarely pre-train from scratch—they inherit a base model. The pre-training objective determines whether the model is natively generative (CLM) or needs adaptation for open-ended output. Instruction tuning and RLHF build on this foundation; weak pre-training cannot be fully fixed later.
How it works
Causal LM loss
For tokens
, minimize negative log-likelihood:
Each step applies softmax over vocabulary logits from the final hidden state at position
Data and compute

Training runs on trillions of tokens from web, books, code, and curated filters. Batches pack sequences; learning rate warms up then decays; mixed precision (bf16/fp16) and distributed data parallel span thousands of GPUs. None of this runs in typical agent deployment— but sets the capability ceiling.
Trade-offs by objective
CLM: best for generation and agents; unidirectional context during pre-training
MLM: strong encoders; not directly autoregressive
Mixture of sources: code-heavy pre-training improves tool-use; too much low-quality web hurts alignment
Real-world example
A code-heavy base model (pre-trained on GitHub-like corpora) produces valid JSON and API idioms more often than a text-only base—reducing agent parsing retries even before fine-tuning on tool traces.
Common mistakes
Treating base and instruct checkpoints interchangeably for agents
Assuming pre-training encodes up-to-date facts—it is a snapshot with cutoff
Underestimating data cleaning impact on toxicity and bias in downstream agents
Key takeaways
Pre-training objectives: CLM dominates agent-facing LLMs
Loss is summed cross-entropy over token predictions
Corpus mix shapes code, reasoning, and safety priors
Instruction tuning and RLHF refine behavior; they do not replace pre-training
